description:
You are acting as an automation-workflow specialist who has just been given an application event log that lives on the local machine.  
Your goal is to derive two compact CSV reports from that raw log, using only standard GNU/Linux command-line utilities (e.g. grep, awk, cut, sort, uniq).  The whole job should require no more than three or four individual commands in total.

Initial material that already exists on disk  
• Raw log file:  
  /home/user/projects/logs/raw_events.log

Log-line format (already in the file)  
Each non-blank record is a single line that looks exactly like:  
EVENT|<ISO-8601-timestamp>|<username>|<action>

Example (this exact string is in the file):  
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:12:45Z|alice|LOGIN_SUCCESS

Task – produce two new CSV files, both inside /home/user/projects/logs/  

1. login_success_events.csv  
   • Must contain only the records whose action field equals the literal string LOGIN_SUCCESS.  
   • Output fields and order: timestamp,user  
   • First row is the header line:  
     timestamp,user  
   • Followed by one row per matching log line, sorted in ascending (earliest→latest) timestamp order.  
   • Each data row must look like:  
     2023-09-01T10:12:45Z,alice  

2. login_success_counts.csv  
   • Must show how many LOGIN_SUCCESS events each user generated.  
   • First row is the header line:  
     user,count  
   • One row per distinct user, sorted primarily by descending count (largest→smallest).  
     If two users have the same count, sort those ties alphabetically by the username so
     the file order is fully deterministic.  
   • Each data row must be the username followed by its integer count, separated by a single comma, for example:  
     alice,2  

Formatting rules checked by the grader  
• Both output files must use Unix line endings (LF only).  
• No blank lines or trailing spaces are allowed anywhere in either file.  
• The header rows are case-sensitive and must match exactly what is written above.  
• File names and their location are case-sensitive; the test harness will look specifically at  
  /home/user/projects/logs/login_success_events.csv and  
  /home/user/projects/logs/login_success_counts.csv.  

When you are finished, the only observable changes to the filesystem should be the creation of those two new CSV files with the precise contents and order described.  
Do NOT rename, move, or delete the original raw_events.log file.  No additional files or directories should be created.  

A short confirmation message on stdout is fine, but avoid printing the full contents of the CSV files in the terminal—the grader will read the files directly.
truth:
Initial file that is already present before the agent begins:
/home/user/projects/logs/raw_events.log
Contents (10 lines, each ending with LF):
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:12:45Z|alice|LOGIN_SUCCESS
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:15:01Z|bob|LOGIN_FAILURE
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:17:22Z|alice|FILE_UPLOAD
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:20:55Z|carol|LOGIN_SUCCESS
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:25:30Z|bob|LOGIN_SUCCESS
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:30:05Z|alice|LOGIN_SUCCESS
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:35:47Z|dave|FILE_DELETE
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:40:18Z|carol|LOGIN_FAILURE
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:45:00Z|bob|LOGIN_SUCCESS
EVENT|2023-09-01T10:50:10Z|alice|LOGIN_FAILURE

Files that must exist when the agent has successfully completed the task:

1. /home/user/projects/logs/login_success_events.csv
Exact contents (6 lines, each ending with LF):
timestamp,user
2023-09-01T10:12:45Z,alice
2023-09-01T10:20:55Z,carol
2023-09-01T10:25:30Z,bob
2023-09-01T10:30:05Z,alice
2023-09-01T10:45:00Z,bob

2. /home/user/projects/logs/login_success_counts.csv
Exact contents (4 lines, each ending with LF):
user,count
alice,2
bob,2
carol,1

Permissions: the /home/user/projects/logs directory already exists and is writable by the user running the agent (0700 on the directory is acceptable).  No special permissions are required for the new CSV files; default umask is fine.

No other changes to the filesystem should occur.
